threat_model: Naive-observer adversary: sees only GPU-visible tensors produced by the masked forward (post-mask Linear inputs / outputs and the activation island's permuted input). Has prior knowledge of the plaintext channel distribution but does NOT execute adaptive or learned inversion attacks, does NOT observe trusted-side tensors, and does NOT use side channels.

fixed_permutation.expected_risk_level: high (stable across sessions; aggregate signature is sharp)
fixed_permutation.interpretation: Fixed permutation lets the attacker accumulate samples across sessions; the per-channel signature converges to the true permuted distribution and greedy matching recovers above chance.

fresh_permutation_per_session.expected_risk_level: moderate (per-session recovery only; no cross-session alignment)
fresh_permutation_per_session.interpretation: Fresh permutation breaks cross-session alignment, so the attacker only has M = samples_per_session points per per-session signature.

permutation_pool.expected_risk_level: moderate-to-high (per-perm signature is sharper than fresh but weaker than fixed)
permutation_pool.interpretation: Each pool entry gets sessions_per_perm × M samples — between fixed and fresh in signature sharpness.

dense_sandwich_reference.expected_risk_level: near random chance (column statistics destroyed by dense mixing)
dense_sandwich_reference.interpretation: Dense linear mixing on both sides of the permutation destroys the per-channel signature; recovery drops to ~ 1/H random chance.

fixed_perm_no_pad.interpretation: Same plaintext + same permutation → identical GPU-visible tensor every request; pairwise cosine collapses to 1.

fixed_perm_with_linear_boundary_pad.interpretation: Fresh pad and mask at the Linear boundary collapse linkability on (X - T) N_in, but the activation input Z P remains identical across requests because the permutation is fixed.

fresh_perm_with_linear_boundary_pad.interpretation: Fresh permutation per trial scrambles the activation input as well; cosine across trials drops because Z P_i ≠ Z P_j.

dense_to_perm_to_dense_sandwich.interpretation: Fresh dense masks around the permutation island destroy coordinate-value alignment; the post-island visible tensor has near-zero pairwise cosine across requests.

main_metric_per_strategy.metric: mean_pairwise_cosine over the principal visible view

notes: Activation island permutation preserves the coordinate-value multiset.
Boundary pad does not protect the activation input when P is fixed.
Dense sandwich is the strongest of the four under naive observers.

table.correctness_role: right-multiply mask for arbitrary invertible linear ops
table.preserved_statistics: rank, dimension, algebraic relations under same mask
table.gpu_visible_leakage: channel identity hidden via dense mixing; algebraic structure preserved only under reuse
table.mitigation: fresh mask across sessions; pad at Linear boundaries
table.notes: Strongest of the listed families; the reference against which the others are compared.

table.correctness_role: commutes with rms(.) and right-rotates the normalised state
table.preserved_statistics: row L2 norm (||X N||_2 = ||X||_2); pairwise dot products if the same mask is reused
table.gpu_visible_leakage: row L2 norm preserved by design; coordinate identity hidden under rotation
table.mitigation: restrict island lifetime; sandwich with dense masks at Linear boundaries
table.notes: Designed to preserve rms; an attacker observing many samples can always read row norms.

table.correctness_role: commutes with LayerNorm core (mean + centered variance preserved)
table.preserved_statistics: row mean (X N · 1 = X · 1); row centered L2 norm
table.gpu_visible_leakage: row mean and centered norm preserved by design
table.mitigation: sandwich with dense masks at Linear boundaries; avoid reuse across sessions
table.notes: Strict subset of orthogonal masks: also preserves the all-ones direction.

table.correctness_role: commutes exactly with element-wise activations: f(X P) = f(X) P
table.gpu_visible_leakage: channel identity hidden if P is secret; multiset always leaks
table.mitigation: fresh permutation per session; permutation pool; dense sandwich at Linear boundaries
table.security_strength_relative_to_dense: weaker (multiset and sorted values always preserved)
table.notes: Permutation islands hide channel identity but do not hide coordinate-value multisets.

table.correctness_role: commutes with SwiGLU when up and gate use the same P
table.preserved_statistics: coordinate-value multiset; paired (up, gate) alignment per channel
table.gpu_visible_leakage: same as permutation, plus paired alignment exposes that up and gate share P
table.mitigation: fresh paired permutation; branch-consistency checks; dense sandwich
table.notes: Inherits permutation leakage; the paired structure is an additional small leakage channel.

summary_notes: Compatible mask families are weaker than unrestricted dense masks inside nonlinear islands.
Permutation islands hide channel identity but do not hide coordinate-value multisets.
Orthogonal masks preserve row norms by design.
Mean-preserving orthogonal masks preserve row means and centered norms by design.
Dense sandwiching and pad at Linear boundaries are required mitigations.

limitations: These experiments are security proxies, not formal security proofs.
Compatible mask families are weaker than unrestricted dense masks inside nonlinear islands.
Permutation islands hide channel identity but do not hide coordinate-value multisets.
Orthogonal masks preserve row norms by design.
Mean-preserving orthogonal masks preserve row means and centered norms by design.
Fresh permutation reduces stable statistical recovery but does not provide dense linear mixing.
Dense sandwiching and pad at Linear boundaries are required mitigations.
This stage does not implement adaptive learned inversion attacks.
This stage does not implement real TEE isolation.
This stage does not prove semantic security.
